turns.content: I'm trying to build a lightweight event notification system for my app. I was thinking I could just use thread::spawn for the background task and then manually call thread::unpark on the main thread whenever something happens, but I think I might be missing something about how this actually works.

You're close to the right idea. The core mechanism is that thread::spawn creates a new thread of execution, while thread::park and thread::unpark are used for synchronization between threads. When you call thread::park on a thread, it enters a sleeping state until unparked by another thread. If you want to build an event system where the background task wakes up the main thread or vice versa, you need to ensure one side is parked and the other calls unpark.
So I should have my worker thread calling thread::unpark whenever it has an event? But what if the main thread isn't sleeping at all?

Exactly. The pattern is that one thread needs to be parked. If your main thread isn't sleeping, it won't wait for the unpark call. You could use a channel for signaling events instead of raw synchronization primitives, but if you want to avoid channels and keep it simple with just threads, then yes, the worker thread should call thread::unpark whenever there's an event ready.
Okay, so I'm parking my main thread in a loop. But what about thread::yield_now? Is that relevant here?

thread::yield_now is useful for cooperative multitasking when you don't want to block the thread entirely, but it's not part of the park/unpark synchronization pattern. For your event system, stick with thread::park and thread::unpark if you need true waiting behavior.
I'm still confused about how this compares to Condvar. Should I use that instead?

Condvar is a higher-level abstraction built on top of parking and unparking threads internally, but it requires you to manage the mutex around the condition variable yourself. If you just want raw thread synchronization without extra overhead or complexity, thread::park and thread::unpair are more direct.
Got it. So if I'm building a custom event system for performance reasons, I should avoid Condvar? But what about the atomic types like AtomicBool or AtomicUsize?

Atomic types are useful for flag-based signaling where you don't need full synchronization primitives. For your event system, if you want to keep things simple and avoid complex locking, thread::park/unpair is the way to go without needing atomic variables.

Wait a minute, so my main thread calls thread::park in a loop, checks for an event flag, and if there's no event it just sleeps? And when the worker has data, it sets the flag and calls thread::unpark?

Exactly. You park your main thread in a loop, check for an event flag, and if there's no event you just sleep or yield. When the worker has data to send, it sets the flag and calls thread::unpark on your parked thread, waking it up immediately.

I've been using Reactor patterns in Node.js where the event loop blocks until something happens, but I'm trying to replicate this in Rust with threads. When I try to make a thread wait for an event without blocking my whole process, what's the right primitive?

You're looking at thread::park and thread::unpairing. In your scenario, one thread will call thread::park to sleep until another thread calls thread::unpark on it. This is distinct from Condvar because you don't need to lock a mutex around the wait; the park/unpark pair itself provides the synchronization without extra overhead.
That sounds elegant, but I'm worried about deadlocks or forgetting to unpark. Is there a way to make this safer than manually calling thread::unpark every time?

You can wrap the park/unpark logic in an event struct that holds a pointer to the thread handle and a boolean flag for whether it's been unparked. When your worker thread detects an event, it sets that flag and calls thread::unpark on the target handle instead of holding locks. This pattern prevents deadlocks because you never acquire a lock while waiting.
I tried building this manually but my CI pipeline keeps failing with spurious wakeups or lost messages. What's the common mistake I'm making?

The most common mistake is trying to use thread::park without ensuring that every call to park has a corresponding unpark, or you're forgetting to check if an unpark was successful before proceeding. You must ensure the event flag is set before calling thread::unpark so the waiting thread actually wakes up.
Okay, I added a guard to check if the flag is set before calling thread::unpark. But now my pipeline still fails intermittently. What else could be wrong?

You likely forgot that thread::park can return early due to spurious wakeups, so you need a loop around the park call rather than a single statement. Your code should check if the wait succeeded before assuming the event arrived.

I'm trying to build a lightweight event system for my tool, but I keep getting confused by thread::park and thread::unpark versus the older Condvar approach. What's the actual difference between these two mechanisms in terms of how they handle waking threads?

The core distinction is that Condvar is a synchronization primitive specifically designed to allow one thread to signal multiple waiting threads, whereas thread::park and thread::unpark are lower-level primitives meant for single-threaded signaling. If you try to use thread::unpark on multiple waiters without extra bookkeeping, it won't wake them all at once like Condvar does.
Oh, so I thought thread::unpark would just work like a broadcast signal. Can you clarify how many threads can actually be parked with the same unpark call? Does it wake all of them or just one?

thread::unpark wakes only a single waiting thread, not a group. If multiple threads are parked on the same variable, you must manually track which ones need to be signaled and call unpark individually for each one that has changed state.

That's a big deal because I was assuming it would handle batching automatically. So if I'm building a lightweight event system, do I have to manage the list of waiting threads myself when using this approach?

Yes, you absolutely must maintain your own state or queue to track which threads are parked and then selectively call unpark on them. This gives you more control but requires extra bookkeeping compared to Condvar's built-in atomic broadcast behavior.

I see, so the trade-off is that thread::park/unpark offers finer-grained control at the cost of manual state management, while Condvar handles the broadcasting out of the box. Is there any scenario where I should prefer one over the other for my event system?

Use thread::park and unpark when you need precise control over which specific threads wake up based on custom logic, like waking only a subset of workers. Choose Condvar when your event needs to notify all waiting threads atomically without managing additional lists or state.
